title: Writing - Part 1: Education Technology
description: Your class has listened to a talk on the growing use of technology in education and the debates surrounding its benefits and drawbacks.

content.question: Write an essay discussing two of the considerations in your notes. You should explain which consideration you think is most important for educators and policymakers to address, giving reasons for your opinion.

opinions: Technology can tailor content to individual learning styles and boost motivation in the classroom.
Excessive screen time has been linked to reduced attention spans and negative impacts on sleep.
Students from lower-income backgrounds often lack reliable devices or internet access at home.

structure.introduction: Briefly introduce the debate over technology in education and indicate which two considerations you will discuss.
structure.bodyParagraph1: Explore the first consideration, illustrating its significance with evidence or examples.
structure.bodyParagraph2: Examine the second consideration and present a reasoned argument for which is more important to address.
structure.conclusion: Summarise the two considerations and reaffirm your view on the priority for educators and policymakers.

content.modelAnswer:
The rapid integration of technology into educational settings has prompted considerable debate, with advocates highlighting its potential to transform learning and critics warning of unintended consequences. Two considerations demand particular attention: the promise of personalised learning and the problem of digital inequality.

Technology offers remarkable possibilities for tailoring education to individual needs. Adaptive learning platforms can analyse a student's performance in real time, adjusting the difficulty and style of content accordingly. This approach has been shown to increase engagement, particularly among students who might otherwise disengage from a one-size-fits-all classroom model. When implemented thoughtfully, educational technology can help close attainment gaps by providing targeted support that a single teacher managing thirty pupils cannot feasibly offer.

However, the benefits of educational technology can only be realised if all students have reliable access to devices and connectivity. At present, this is far from guaranteed. Research consistently shows that children from lower-income households are significantly less likely to have access to a personal device or stable broadband at home, meaning that technology-driven homework tasks or remote learning can deepen rather than narrow existing inequalities. A child unable to complete digital assignments is placed at an immediate disadvantage relative to more privileged peers.

Of these two considerations, digital inequality is the more pressing. Personalised learning represents an aspiration; unequal access represents a barrier that prevents the aspiration from being realised at all. Until governments and schools ensure equitable access to devices and connectivity, the potential of educational technology will remain unevenly distributed.
